title: Is fasting worth it? With Georgi Dinkov

chunks.title: Introduction to Podcast and Guests
chunks.summary: The podcast introduces the guest, George Dinkov, and emphasizes the topics that will be discussed, including carbohydrate risks, amino acids, and the impacts of fasting. The host expresses excitement about the conversation and encourages listeners to engage with prior episodes.
chunks.transcript: I don't know if any story that showed if you keep the Blue Cross below this level all this Bonanza of benefits will will uh you know materialize I I don't know if I've seen those studies on this week's podcast I had the pleasure of talking to George dinkov for the third time every time we do this I learn a lot it gets to be kind of technical at times but bear with us and we both try to break it down and make it digestible for all of you we get into things like carbohydrate benefits versus risks we get into polyunsaturated fatty acids in cell membranes and the real cause of diabetes we talk about fasting is it good is it bad should you take Metformin what are the real root causes of cancer and more this is an amazing podcast and I think you will learn a ton from this you can find Georgie at heydude h-a-i-d-u-t dot me that's his blog and he posts those things on twitters but there are three episodes of Swords Georgie you can go back and listen to the first two if you want the full breath of our conversation but again this is a really fun conversation I think you guys will learn a ton here all right guys that's it on to the podcast enjoy this one with Georgie dinkov I'm

chunks.title: Debating Longevity and Fasting
chunks.summary: The episode delves into the controversial views surrounding longevity strategies, particularly the idea that fasting is essential for long life. George presents the argument that it might not be calorie restriction, but rather specific nutrient intake, that affects lifespan.
chunks.transcript: this question so there's a lot of talk right now in the health space about longevity and the main I would say the main Paradigm that the mainly accepted idea is that the way to live to be kind of a Brazen fashion go so far as to suggest kind of I don't know they suggest medications like metformin like everyone should be on Metformin and rapamycin and for people don't know this is with your perspective on that longevity strategy and then maybe we can talk about fasting and we can talk about metformin because these are all the rage today and I think that there's an important counter position to consider there I mean if you look at the study it's really interesting it's become another political topic uh it actually fits right into the whole idea of of reducing carbon footprint and like eating less right getting by on fewer calories and whatnot and the way to sell it to the population I think they they kind of stumbled upon this finding from the early 60s where uh different groups demonstrated that if you consume less calories you're going to extend both the the average and the maximum lifespan uh however what the current political uh interest around this idea doesn't tell is that the same groups actually said I know it can't be just the calories let's let's find out and investigate what exactly is causing the increasing lifespan and they found out that you can achieve the exact same extension even if in fact even further a larger extension of the maximum lifespan by by restricting specific amino acids upon the diet you can eat as many calories as you want right in fact some of the animals they had these amino acid restrictors they kept eating even two times or three times more calories they did not gain weight and they still live longer so the whole thing about eating less and suffering in order for you to live longer I think it goes back to this idea that for some reason the Western societies have are stuck on it is basically no pain nor gain no pain no gain right um and also this this kind of morphed into even more uh perverse idea

chunks.title: Debunking Caloric Restriction Myths
chunks.summary: The conversation examines studies from the 1960s that challenged the notion of caloric restriction as the primary means to extend life. George explains that the key to longevity might be in nutrient quality rather than sheer calorie counts.
chunks.transcript: one is definitely not true about the you know no pain no gain I think there is some validity to that in life but not not necessarily in regards to eating and extending your lifespan um so these groups that did the original research demonstrated that you don't have to restrict the calories you can achieve the exact same thing by by restricting some of the nutrients in the food and also they noticed that the fasting the benefits are fasting are mostly due to the DraStic reduction of endotoxin I mean basically when you're eating food whatever it doesn't get digested as we mentioned in the previous podcast it's going to reach a microbiome and the gram-negative portion of it by consuming these undigested Foods is going to increase its turnover and all the bacteria that dice it will rupture and you know release this endotoxin into the initial into column but as subsequent studies demonstrated that over time this leads to a breakdown of the gut barrier so this endotoxins into the bloodstream and creates all kinds of chronic inflammatory problems so these groups back in the 60s knew that it's not the actual calories that really matter it's the quality of the food right so the whole thing about calories in versus calories out that flies out the door you can actually eat a lot more not getting fat and live longer and I think the uh the one the the specific

chunks.title: Fasting and Inflammation
chunks.summary: George describes fasting as a potential way to reduce inflammation due to lower endotoxin levels in the gut. However, he cautions that not everyone benefits from fasting equally, particularly older adults with chronic health issues.

chunks.title: The Role of Exercise and Muscle Mass
chunks.summary: The discussion shifts gears towards exercise, highlighting that high-intensity training might be more beneficial than long-duration workouts. George notes the importance of maintaining muscle mass for overall health and longevity.
chunks.transcript: right but but if you're making more effort in fact drastically more uh just that's how nature is structured the curve is logarithmic you're going to get a drastically diminished Returns on your um you know effort of investment you know last week I did a podcast with Dave asprey and it was interesting because we talked about exercise specifically and we didn't really get into this idea in detail of um like a high intensity interval training but I think that there's some research that like five minutes total per week of of high intensity exercise leads to equivalent or better cardiovascular fitness than than six hours of training one hour of running every day yep yeah yeah yeah so the the recommendation for I believe most people was hey you can get a lot of cardiovascular fitness by doing something that is highly intense and if that's climbing three flights of stairs for you that's that's great or if you can Sprint then you can do Sprints and so I thought wow like that really flies in the face of our underlying sort of overarching puritanical perspective as westernized Americans like I Must Destroy myself to be fit if you can go out and do one Sprint like for 30 seconds a day like a few times a week and get pretty significant cardiovascular fitness from a full out Sprint that's wild and yeah and if you can't physically do a Sprint you can get on an exercise bike and go really hard or you can just walk up three flights of stairs or you know it's interesting because I think there are people at different levels of exertional capacity and they may not have the mobility to get like full aerobic capacity but getting people to like really intense exercise for short amounts of time I love that concept it's like oh you don't have to just Crush yourself just go do one hard thing you know for 20 seconds once a day it's probably pretty pretty beneficial and obviously I'm not that's not the specific programming but I think Beyond you don't need to get crazy in

chunks.title: Final Thoughts and Health Recommendations
chunks.summary: George summarizes his views on diet, recommending a return to traditional eating patterns free from excess PUFA and emphasizing nutrient-rich foods. He encourages listeners to enjoy their meals and lead a balanced lifestyle for optimal health.
